question: 2. Find every real-valued function \( f \) whose domain is an interval \( I \) (finite or infinite) having 0 as a left-hand endpoint, such that for every positive member \( x \) of \( I \) the average of \( f \) over the closed interval \( [0, x] \) is equal to the geometric mean of the numbers \( f(0) \) and \( f(x) \).
solution:
Solution. Since the geometric mean is defined only for positive numbers, such a function must be everywhere positive; moreover, it must be integrable on \( [0, x] \) for every \( x \) in its domain.

Put \( f(0)=a \) for convenience and let
\[
F(x)=\int_{0}^{x} f(t) d t
\]

Then the condition of the problem is equivalent to
\[
a f(x)=\left(\frac{1}{x} F(x)\right)^{2}
\]
for all positive \( x \in I \).
Now (1) shows that \( F \) is continuous, so for \( x>0, f \) is continuous by (2) and \( F \) is then differentiable by (1). Then (2) becomes
\[
a F^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{x^{2}} F(x)^{2} .
\]

We can integrate this differential equation by separating the variables, and we find
\[
F(x)=\frac{a x}{1-c x},
\]
where \( c \) is the constant of integration. Therefore
\[
f(x)=F^{\prime}(x)=\frac{a}{(1-c x)^{2}} .
\]
for \( x>0 \). We note that this formula remains valid for \( \boldsymbol{x}=0 \). If \( \boldsymbol{c}>0 \), then \( f \) is not integrable on \( [0,1 / c] \), hence we see that every solution is given by
\[
f(x)=\frac{a}{(1-c x)^{2}}\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
\text { for } 0 \leq x<\frac{1}{c}, & \text { if } c>0 \\
\text { for } 0 \leq x<\infty, & \text { if } c \leq 0
\end{array}\right.
\]
where \( a>0 \).
It is readily checked that all functions defined by (3) are solutions of the problem.

Remark. If \( \approx<0 \). then (3) gives solutions of (2) that are not solutions of the problem. If \( a=0 \), then (2) has many more solutions, namely, any function that is zero almost everywhere on \( \mid 0, \infty) \).

descriptive_long.question: 2. Find every real-valued function \( realfunc \) whose domain is an interval \( intervali \) (finite or infinite) having 0 as a left-hand endpoint, such that for every positive member \( variable \) of \( intervali \) the average of \( realfunc \) over the closed interval \( [0, variable] \) is equal to the geometric mean of the numbers \( realfunc(0) \) and \( realfunc(variable) \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Since the geometric mean is defined only for positive numbers, such a function must be everywhere positive; moreover, it must be integrable on \( [0, variable] \) for every \( variable \) in its domain.

Put \( realfunc(0)=constanta \) for convenience and let
\[
integral(variable)=\int_{0}^{variable} realfunc(dummyvar) \, d dummyvar
\]
Then the condition of the problem is equivalent to
\[
constanta \, realfunc(variable)=\left(\frac{1}{variable} integral(variable)\right)^{2}
\]
for all positive \( variable \in intervali \).
Now (1) shows that \( integral \) is continuous, so for \( variable>0, realfunc \) is continuous by (2) and \( integral \) is then differentiable by (1). Then (2) becomes
\[
constanta \, integral^{\prime}(variable)=\frac{1}{variable^{2}} integral(variable)^{2} .
\]
We can integrate this differential equation by separating the variables, and we find
\[
integral(variable)=\frac{constanta \, variable}{1-constantc \, variable},
\]
where \( constantc \) is the constant of integration. Therefore
\[
realfunc(variable)=integral^{\prime}(variable)=\frac{constanta}{(1-constantc \, variable)^{2}} .
\]
for \( variable>0 \). We note that this formula remains valid for \( \boldsymbol{variable}=0 \). If \( \boldsymbol{constantc}>0 \), then \( realfunc \) is not integrable on \( [0,1 / constantc] \); hence we see that every solution is given by
\[
realfunc(variable)=\frac{constanta}{(1-constantc \, variable)^{2}}\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
\text { for } 0 \leq variable<\frac{1}{constantc}, & \text { if } constantc>0 \\
\text { for } 0 \leq variable<\infty, & \text { if } constantc \leq 0
\end{array}\right.
\]
where \( constanta>0 \).
It is readily checked that all functions defined by (3) are solutions of the problem.

Remark. If \( constantc<0 \), then (3) gives solutions of (2) that are not solutions of the problem. If \( constanta=0 \), then (2) has many more solutions, namely, any function that is zero almost everywhere on \( \mid 0, \infty) \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 2. Find every real-valued function \( marigold \) whose domain is an interval \( sunflower \) (finite or infinite) having 0 as a left-hand endpoint, such that for every positive member \( constellation \) of \( sunflower \) the average of \( marigold \) over the closed interval \( [0, constellation] \) is equal to the geometric mean of the numbers \( marigold(0) \) and \( marigold(constellation) \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Since the geometric mean is defined only for positive numbers, such a function must be everywhere positive; moreover, it must be integrable on \( [0, constellation] \) for every \( constellation \) in its domain.

Put \( marigold(0)=porcelain \) for convenience and let
\[
thunderbolt(constellation)=\int_{0}^{constellation} marigold(lemonade) d lemonade
\]

Then the condition of the problem is equivalent to
\[
porcelain \, marigold(constellation)=\left(\frac{1}{constellation} thunderbolt(constellation)\right)^{2}
\]
for all positive \( constellation \in sunflower \).
Now (1) shows that \( thunderbolt \) is continuous, so for \( constellation>0, marigold \) is continuous by (2) and \( thunderbolt \) is then differentiable by (1). Then (2) becomes
\[
porcelain \, thunderbolt^{\prime}(constellation)=\frac{1}{constellation^{2}} thunderbolt(constellation)^{2} .
\]

We can integrate this differential equation by separating the variables, and we find
\[
thunderbolt(constellation)=\frac{porcelain \, constellation}{1-willowtree \, constellation},
\]
where \( willowtree \) is the constant of integration. Therefore
\[
marigold(constellation)=thunderbolt^{\prime}(constellation)=\frac{porcelain}{(1-willowtree \, constellation)^{2}} .
\]
for \( constellation>0 \). We note that this formula remains valid for \( \boldsymbol{constellation}=0 \). If \( \boldsymbol{willowtree}>0 \), then \( marigold \) is not integrable on \( [0,1 / willowtree] \), hence we see that every solution is given by
\[
marigold(constellation)=\frac{porcelain}{(1-willowtree \, constellation)^{2}}\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
\text { for } 0 \leq constellation<\frac{1}{willowtree}, & \text { if } willowtree>0 \\
\text { for } 0 \leq constellation<\infty, & \text { if } willowtree \leq 0
\end{array}\right.
\]
where \( porcelain>0 \).
It is readily checked that all functions defined by (3) are solutions of the problem.

Remark. If \( \approx<0 \). then (3) gives solutions of (2) that are not solutions of the problem. If \( porcelain=0 \), then (2) has many more solutions, namely, any function that is zero almost everywhere on \( \mid 0, \infty) \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 2. Find every real-valued function \( constant \) whose domain is an interval \( pointset \) (finite or infinite) having 0 as a left-hand endpoint, such that for every positive member \( steadfast \) of \( pointset \) the average of \( constant \) over the closed interval \( [0, steadfast] \) is equal to the geometric mean of the numbers \( constant(0) \) and \( constant(steadfast) \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Since the geometric mean is defined only for positive numbers, such a function must be everywhere positive; moreover, it must be integrable on \( [0, steadfast] \) for every \( steadfast \) in its domain.

Put \( constant(0)=negative \) for convenience and let
\[
derivative(steadfast)=\int_{0}^{steadfast} constant(permanent) d permanent
\]
Then the condition of the problem is equivalent to
\[
negative\; constant(steadfast)=\left(\frac{1}{steadfast} derivative(steadfast)\right)^{2}
\]
for all positive \( steadfast \in pointset \).
Now (1) shows that \( derivative \) is continuous, so for \( steadfast>0, constant \) is continuous by (2) and \( derivative \) is then differentiable by (1). Then (2) becomes
\[
negative\; derivative^{\prime}(steadfast)=\frac{1}{steadfast^{2}} derivative(steadfast)^{2} .
\]
We can integrate this differential equation by separating the variables, and we find
\[
derivative(steadfast)=\frac{negative\; steadfast}{1-variable\; steadfast},
\]
where \( variable \) is the constant of integration. Therefore
\[
constant(steadfast)=derivative^{\prime}(steadfast)=\frac{negative}{(1-variable\; steadfast)^{2}} .
\]
for \( steadfast>0 \). We note that this formula remains valid for \( \boldsymbol{steadfast}=0 \). If \( \boldsymbol{variable}>0 \), then \( constant \) is not integrable on \( [0,1 / variable] \), hence we see that every solution is given by
\[
constant(steadfast)=\frac{negative}{(1-variable\; steadfast)^{2}}\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
\text { for } 0 \leq steadfast<\frac{1}{variable}, & \text { if } variable>0 \\
\text { for } 0 \leq steadfast<\infty, & \text { if } variable \leq 0
\end{array}\right.
\]
where \( negative>0 \).
It is readily checked that all functions defined by (3) are solutions of the problem.

Remark. If \( \approx<0 \). then (3) gives solutions of (2) that are not solutions of the problem. If \( negative=0 \), then (2) has many more solutions, namely, any function that is zero almost everywhere on \( \mid 0, \infty) \).

garbled_string.question: 2. Find every real-valued function \( qzxwvtnp \) whose domain is an interval \( asdfghjk \) (finite or infinite) having 0 as a left-hand endpoint, such that for every positive member \( hjgrksla \) of \( asdfghjk \) the average of \( qzxwvtnp \) over the closed interval \( [0, hjgrksla] \) is equal to the geometric mean of the numbers \( qzxwvtnp(0) \) and \( qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Since the geometric mean is defined only for positive numbers, such a function must be everywhere positive; moreover, it must be integrable on \( [0, hjgrksla] \) for every \( hjgrksla \) in its domain.

Put \( qzxwvtnp(0)=zxcvbnma \) for convenience and let
\[
plmoknji(hjgrksla)=\int_{0}^{hjgrksla} qzxwvtnp(mnbvcxse) d mnbvcxse
\]

Then the condition of the problem is equivalent to
\[
zxcvbnma \, qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla)=\left(\frac{1}{hjgrksla} plmoknji(hjgrksla)\right)^{2}
\]
for all positive \( hjgrksla \in asdfghjk \).
Now (1) shows that \( plmoknji \) is continuous, so for \( hjgrksla>0, qzxwvtnp \) is continuous by (2) and \( plmoknji \) is then differentiable by (1). Then (2) becomes
\[
zxcvbnma \, plmoknji^{\prime}(hjgrksla)=\frac{1}{hjgrksla^{2}} plmoknji(hjgrksla)^{2} .
\]

We can integrate this differential equation by separating the variables, and we find
\[
plmoknji(hjgrksla)=\frac{zxcvbnma \, hjgrksla}{1-qwertyui \, hjgrksla},
\]
where \( qwertyui \) is the constant of integration. Therefore
\[
qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla)=plmoknji^{\prime}(hjgrksla)=\frac{zxcvbnma}{(1-qwertyui \, hjgrksla)^{2}} .
\]
for \( hjgrksla>0 \). We note that this formula remains valid for \( \boldsymbol{hjgrksla}=0 \). If \( \boldsymbol{qwertyui}>0 \), then \( qzxwvtnp \) is not integrable on \( [0,1 / qwertyui] \), hence we see that every solution is given by
\[
qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla)=\frac{zxcvbnma}{(1-qwertyui \, hjgrksla)^{2}}\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
\text { for } 0 \leq hjgrksla<\frac{1}{qwertyui}, & \text { if } qwertyui>0 \\
\text { for } 0 \leq hjgrksla<\infty, & \text { if } qwertyui \leq 0
\end{array}\right.
\]
where \( zxcvbnma>0 \).
It is readily checked that all functions defined by (3) are solutions of the problem.

Remark. If \( \approx<0 \). then (3) gives solutions of (2) that are not solutions of the problem. If \( zxcvbnma=0 \), then (2) has many more solutions, namely, any function that is zero almost everywhere on \( \mid 0, \infty) \).

kernel_variant.question:
Fix a positive real parameter \alpha  > 0 (\alpha  need not be an integer).  
Let I be an interval whose left-hand end-point is 1, i.e.  

  I = [1, b) with 1 < b \leq  \infty  .  

Determine every continuously differentiable and strictly positive function  

  f : I \to  (0, \infty )

that fulfils, for every x \in  I with x > 1, the weighted-mean identity  

           \alpha                                     
     --------------------------------- \int ^1x t^{\alpha -1} f(t)\,dt = ( f(1)^{\alpha }\,f(x) )^{1/(\alpha +1)} . (\star )  
     x^{\alpha } - 1                                    

(Geometrically: the \alpha -dimensional radial average of f on the shell 1 \leq  t \leq  x
equals the geometric mean in which f(1) appears \alpha  times and f(x) once.)

Describe explicitly all such functions and, for each of them, the maximal
sub-interval of I on which it is defined.
kernel_variant.solution:
Put  

  c := f(1) > 0,  F(x) := \int _1x t^{\alpha -1} f(t)\,dt  (x > 1). (1)

Because f is C^1 and positive, F is C^1 and strictly increasing with  

  F'(x) = x^{\alpha -1} f(x).               (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Rewriting the functional identity  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Equation (\star ) rewrites via (1) as  

  \alpha  F(x) = (x^{\alpha } - 1) [c^{\alpha } f(x)]^{1/(\alpha +1)}.    (3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Eliminating f(x)  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Raise (3) to the power \alpha +1 and use (2):

\alpha ^{\alpha +1} F(x)^{\alpha +1}
 = (x^{\alpha } - 1)^{\alpha +1} c^{\alpha } f(x)
 = (x^{\alpha } - 1)^{\alpha +1} c^{\alpha } \cdot  F'(x) / x^{\alpha -1}.   (4)

Hence  

  F'(x) = K x^{\alpha -1} (x^{\alpha } - 1)^{-(\alpha +1)} F(x)^{\alpha +1}, (5)

with K := \alpha ^{\alpha +1}/c^{\alpha } > 0.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Separation of variables  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because F > 0 we may divide by F^{\alpha +1}:

  F^{-(\alpha +1)} dF = K x^{\alpha -1}(x^{\alpha } - 1)^{-(\alpha +1)} dx. (6)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Integrating  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Left side: \int  F^{-(\alpha +1)}dF = -F^{-\alpha }/\alpha .        (7)

Right side: let u = x^{\alpha }, so du = \alpha x^{\alpha -1}dx:

\int  x^{\alpha -1}(x^{\alpha } - 1)^{-(\alpha +1)}dx
= (1/\alpha )\int _{1}^{x^{\alpha }} (u - 1)^{-(\alpha +1)}du
= -(x^{\alpha } - 1)^{-\alpha }/\alpha ^2.            (8)

Insert (7)-(8) into (6):

-F^{-\alpha }/\alpha  = -(K/\alpha ^2)(x^{\alpha } - 1)^{-\alpha } + C.     (9)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Solving for F  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Multiply by -\alpha  and write D := -\alpha C:

  F(x)^{-\alpha } = (K/\alpha )(x^{\alpha } - 1)^{-\alpha } + D.    (10)

Since K/\alpha  = \alpha ^{\alpha }/c^{\alpha }, (10) becomes

  F(x)^{-\alpha } = (\alpha ^{\alpha }/c^{\alpha })[(x^{\alpha } - 1)^{-\alpha } + \lambda ], (11)

where we set  

  \lambda  := D c^{\alpha }/\alpha ^{\alpha }  (\lambda  \in  \mathbb{R}).        (12)

Taking the -\alpha -th power yields  

  F(x) = (c/\alpha )(x^{\alpha } - 1)[1 + \lambda (x^{\alpha } - 1)^{\alpha }]^{-1/\alpha }. (13)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Extracting f  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put s := x^{\alpha } - 1 (so ds/dx = \alpha x^{\alpha -1}).  From (13)

F(x) = (c/\alpha ) s (1 + \lambda  s^{\alpha })^{-1/\alpha },

hence  

  F'(x)= c x^{\alpha -1}(1 + \lambda  s^{\alpha })^{-1/\alpha -1}.      (14)

Comparing with (2) furnishes  

  f(x) = c [1 + \lambda (x^{\alpha } - 1)^{\alpha }]^{-1/\alpha -1}.    (15)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7.  Positivity and maximal domain  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The bracket in (15) must stay positive.

*  If \lambda  \geq  0 it never vanishes, so f extends to the whole semi-infinite
   interval [1, \infty ).

*  If \lambda  < 0 we need  

  1 + \lambda (x^{\alpha } - 1)^{\alpha } > 0  
  \Leftrightarrow  (x^{\alpha } - 1)^{\alpha } < |\lambda |^{-1}  
  \Leftrightarrow  x^{\alpha } - 1 < |\lambda |^{-1/\alpha }  
  \Leftrightarrow  x < (1 + |\lambda |^{-1/\alpha })^{1/\alpha }.          (16)

Thus  

  b = \infty  if \lambda  \geq  0,  and  b = (1 + |\lambda |^{-1/\alpha })^{1/\alpha } if \lambda  < 0. (17)

Because the denominator in (15) vanishes at x = b when \lambda  < 0, the right end-point is not included: the solution lives on [1, b).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
8.  Verification  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Substituting (15) into (\star ) (use s = x^{\alpha } - 1 and ds/dx = \alpha x^{\alpha -1}) gives an
identity, so every pair (c, \lambda ) satisfying (17) indeed produces a solution.  
Conversely all solutions have been obtained, because any C^1 positive solution
necessarily leads to the differential equation (5) whose general solution is
(15).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final classification  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
All continuously differentiable positive solutions of (\star ) are  

  f_{c,\lambda }(x) =                         c  
     ---------------------------------------------       x \in  [1,b_{\lambda }),    (18)  
    (1 + \lambda  (x^{\alpha } - 1)^{\alpha })^{1 + 1/\alpha }

where  

 *  c = f(1) > 0 is arbitrary,  
 *  \lambda  \in  \mathbb{R} is arbitrary,  
 *  b_{\lambda } is given by (17).  

For \lambda  \geq  0 we have b_{\lambda }=\infty ; for \lambda  < 0 the maximal interval ends at  
b_{\lambda }=(1+|\lambda |^{-1/\alpha })^{1/\alpha }.  
No other function satisfies the weighted-mean identity (\star ).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher-dimensional weighting:  
 The integral average now involves the weight t^{α−1}, mimicking an α-dimensional volume element.  This forces the solver to cope with a non-uniform kernel and to recognise the substitution u = t^{α}.  

2. Non-integer parameter α and fractional exponents:  
 α is an arbitrary positive real, so the solution must work equally for irrational values.  Handling the power (x^{α}−1)^{−α} and the exponent −1/α requires comfort with general real exponents, not just integers.

3. Non-linear first-order ODE with variable coefficients:  
 Eliminating the integral leads to the nonlinear equation (5) whose right-hand side depends simultaneously on x and F in a non-trivial way.  Solving it demands separation of variables, a change of variables, and careful tracking of constants.

4. Domain analysis:  
 Because the denominator 1+λ (x^{α}−1)^{α} may vanish, one must analyse where the solution remains positive and hence admissible.  This extra case-work is absent in the original problem.

5. Parameter family of solutions:  
 The final answer involves two independent real parameters (f(1) and λ) and a sharp description of the maximal interval of validity, which is subtler than in the original setting.

All these layers go beyond the original cubic-root identity on a flat interval, making the enhanced variant significantly harder and requiring a deeper, multi-step argument.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix a positive real parameter \alpha  > 0 (\alpha  need not be an integer).  
Let I be an interval whose left-hand end-point is 1, i.e.  

  I = [1, b) with 1 < b \leq  \infty  .  

Determine every continuously differentiable and strictly positive function  

  f : I \to  (0, \infty )

that fulfils, for every x \in  I with x > 1, the weighted-mean identity  

           \alpha                                     
     --------------------------------- \int ^1x t^{\alpha -1} f(t)\,dt = ( f(1)^{\alpha }\,f(x) )^{1/(\alpha +1)} . (\star )  
     x^{\alpha } - 1                                    

(Geometrically: the \alpha -dimensional radial average of f on the shell 1 \leq  t \leq  x
equals the geometric mean in which f(1) appears \alpha  times and f(x) once.)

Describe explicitly all such functions and, for each of them, the maximal
sub-interval of I on which it is defined.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Put

Because f is C^1 and positive, F is C^1 and strictly increasing with

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Equation (\star ) rewrites via (1) as

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Eliminating f(x)  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Raise (3) to the power \alpha +1 and use (2):

\alpha ^{\alpha +1} F(x)^{\alpha +1}
 = (x^{\alpha } - 1)^{\alpha +1} c^{\alpha } f(x)

Hence  

  F'(x) = K x^{\alpha -1} (x^{\alpha } - 1)^{-(\alpha +1)} F(x)^{\alpha +1}, (5)

with K := \alpha ^{\alpha +1}/c^{\alpha } > 0.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Separation of variables  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because F > 0 we may divide by F^{\alpha +1}:

  F^{-(\alpha +1)} dF = K x^{\alpha -1}(x^{\alpha } - 1)^{-(\alpha +1)} dx. (6)

--------------------------------------------------------------------

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Left side: \int  F^{-(\alpha +1)}dF = -F^{-\alpha }/\alpha .        (7)

\int  x^{\alpha -1}(x^{\alpha } - 1)^{-(\alpha +1)}dx
= (1/\alpha )\int _{1}^{x^{\alpha }} (u - 1)^{-(\alpha +1)}du

Insert (7)-(8) into (6):

-F^{-\alpha }/\alpha  = -(K/\alpha ^2)(x^{\alpha } - 1)^{-\alpha } + C.     (9)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Solving for F  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Multiply by -\alpha  and write D := -\alpha C:

  F(x)^{-\alpha } = (K/\alpha )(x^{\alpha } - 1)^{-\alpha } + D.    (10)

Since K/\alpha  = \alpha ^{\alpha }/c^{\alpha }, (10) becomes

  F(x)^{-\alpha } = (\alpha ^{\alpha }/c^{\alpha })[(x^{\alpha } - 1)^{-\alpha } + \lambda ], (11)

where we set  

  \lambda  := D c^{\alpha }/\alpha ^{\alpha }  (\lambda  \in  \mathbb{R}).        (12)

Taking the -\alpha -th power yields  

  F(x) = (c/\alpha )(x^{\alpha } - 1)[1 + \lambda (x^{\alpha } - 1)^{\alpha }]^{-1/\alpha }. (13)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Extracting f  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put s := x^{\alpha } - 1 (so ds/dx = \alpha x^{\alpha -1}).  From (13)

F(x) = (c/\alpha ) s (1 + \lambda  s^{\alpha })^{-1/\alpha },

hence  

  F'(x)= c x^{\alpha -1}(1 + \lambda  s^{\alpha })^{-1/\alpha -1}.      (14)

Comparing with (2) furnishes  

  f(x) = c [1 + \lambda (x^{\alpha } - 1)^{\alpha }]^{-1/\alpha -1}.    (15)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7.  Positivity and maximal domain  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The bracket in (15) must stay positive.

*  If \lambda  \geq  0 it never vanishes, so f extends to the whole semi-infinite

*  If \lambda  < 0 we need  

  1 + \lambda (x^{\alpha } - 1)^{\alpha } > 0  
  \Leftrightarrow  (x^{\alpha } - 1)^{\alpha } < |\lambda |^{-1}

  \Leftrightarrow  x < (1 + |\lambda |^{-1/\alpha })^{1/\alpha }.          (16)

Thus  

  b = \infty  if \lambda  \geq  0,  and  b = (1 + |\lambda |^{-1/\alpha })^{1/\alpha } if \lambda  < 0. (17)

Because the denominator in (15) vanishes at x = b when \lambda  < 0, the right end-point is not included: the solution lives on [1, b).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
8.  Verification  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Substituting (15) into (\star ) (use s = x^{\alpha } - 1 and ds/dx = \alpha x^{\alpha -1}) gives an
identity, so every pair (c, \lambda ) satisfying (17) indeed produces a solution.  
Conversely all solutions have been obtained, because any C^1 positive solution
necessarily leads to the differential equation (5) whose general solution is
(15).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final classification  
--------------------------------------------------------------------
All continuously differentiable positive solutions of (\star ) are  

  f_{c,\lambda }(x) =                         c  
     ---------------------------------------------       x \in  [1,b_{\lambda }),    (18)  
    (1 + \lambda  (x^{\alpha } - 1)^{\alpha })^{1 + 1/\alpha }

where  

 *  c = f(1) > 0 is arbitrary,  
 *  \lambda  \in  \mathbb{R} is arbitrary,  
 *  b_{\lambda } is given by (17).  

For \lambda  \geq  0 we have b_{\lambda }=\infty ; for \lambda  < 0 the maximal interval ends at  
b_{\lambda }=(1+|\lambda |^{-1/\alpha })^{1/\alpha }.  
No other function satisfies the weighted-mean identity (\star ).

 The integral average now involves the weight t^{α−1}, mimicking an α-dimensional volume element.  This forces the solver to cope with a non-uniform kernel and to recognise the substitution u = t^{α}.

 Because the denominator 1+λ (x^{α}−1)^{α} may vanish, one must analyse where the solution remains positive and hence admissible.  This extra case-work is absent in the original problem.

5. Parameter family of solutions:  
 The final answer involves two independent real parameters (f(1) and λ) and a sharp description of the maximal interval of validity, which is subtler than in the original setting.

All these layers go beyond the original cubic-root identity on a flat interval, making the enhanced variant significantly harder and requiring a deeper, multi-step argument.
